mnemonics.title: Remembering Quantifier Negation Rules
mnemonics.content: Universal to Existential flip: ∀ becomes ∃, and P becomes ¬P. Think: 'NOT ALL' means 'SOME NOT'. Existential to Universal flip: ∃ becomes ∀, and P becomes ¬P. Think: 'NOT SOME' means 'ALL NOT'.

mnemonics.title: Inference Rules Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: UI = Universal Instantiation: Pulling out a specific case from 'for all'. EI = Existential Instantiation: Picking a representative for 'there exists'. UG = Universal Generalization: Going from one case to 'for all'. EG = Existential Generalization: Going from one case to 'there exists'.

acronyms.usage: Logic system that uses quantifiers and predicates to express statements about objects in a domain
A syntactically correct formula in formal logic
Inference rule: From ∀x P(x), infer P(c)
Inference rule: From P(c) where c is arbitrary, infer ∀x P(x)
Inference rule: From ∃x P(x), infer P(c) for new constant c
Inference rule: From P(c), infer ∃x P(x)

visualTips.visualization: Draw boxes around quantifier scopes. Quantifiers act like 'fences' that capture (bind) variables within their rectangular scope area. Variables inside are 'prisoners', outside are 'free'.
Think of quantifiers as jars. ∀ is a 'ALL jar' - negated means something escaped: ∃. ∃ is a 'EXISTS jar' - negated means jar is empty: ∀. The negation flips the quantifier and the predicate.
Use a funnel diagram: ∀x at top narrows down to specific instance (UI). A ∇ (exists) symbol is like a water drop - expands from one instance to general (EG). UL/EG at top flow down, UG/EI flow up.

analogies.analogy: Think of quantifiers like crowd descriptions. 'Everyone in the class passed' (∀) means every single person. 'Someone in the class got an A' (∃) means at least one person.
Bound variables are like pronouns with antecedents. 'He' is bound to a specific person. Free variables are like undefined pronouns - we don't know who 'he' refers to without more context.
If a sign says 'All items are 50% off' (∀), you can pick any specific item (like a shirt) and know it's 50% off. That's instantiation - going from general to specific.

A valid argument is like a factory machine: if you put correct raw materials (true premises) in, you MUST get the expected product (true conclusion). The machine's design (logical structure) guarantees this.
